title: Mobile Malware
html:
Mobile Malware

A category Yahir studied formally in spring–summer 2025 OSU coursework via Adrienne Felt et al.'s 2011 UC Berkeley survey, screenshot-archived across multiple sessions in May/June 2025. The paper is the empirical baseline: 46 distinct mobile-malware samples from Jan 2009 to June 2011, harvested from anti-virus vendor reports (Symantec, F-Secure, Fortiguard, Lookout, Panda) — split 4 iOS / 24 Symbian / 18 Android. The headline finding (2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 20 pm 44f00f66): all 4 iOS malware samples appeared within a single month in 2009, while Android went from zero to most of its sample count in 2011 alone. iOS won the early mobile-security era by requiring permissions at the right moments — Apple's "annoying in the right places" model is Felt's implicit thesis.

The Felt taxonomy is what makes this concept structural rather than just descriptive: malware sorts by incentive. Novelty/amusement (6/46, predicted to fade as profit motives matured), exfiltration (28/46, location/contacts/IMEI sold downstream for profiling), premium rate SMS (24/46, the dominant cash mechanism), two factor authentication bypass (Spitmo and ZeusMitmo working in tandem with desktop banking trojans — 2FA-via-SMS structurally broken by 2011), credential theft, ad spam, SEO, ransom. The 2008 black-market price sheet pulled out in 2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 26 pm 7f8b8bc5 is the under-cited fact Yahir tags for keeping: bank credentials $10–$1,000, email passwords $4–$30, legitimate ad libraries earn $1.90–$9.50 per user per month — the realistic upper bound on what one user's data is worth. Where's the money? is the right first question for any new vector.

The June 10 Wireshark capture (2025 06 10 screenshot 2025 06 10 at 1 05 42 am f1abc915) — 50+ HTTP GETs from a VMware-NAT VM hitting a /ned.exe endpoint amid normal Windows-update + cert-revocation chatter — is the hands-on companion to the survey: identify the C2 callout in the noise. Same security-shaped semester, same Yahir, doing both the theory and the lab.

The connections worth keeping: the incentive taxonomy is a template that re-applies cleanly to LLM jailbreaks, MCP-server malware, and browser-extension malware. The "iOS won by being annoying at the right moments" pattern slots near disruption as evidence that platform winners often trade UX friction for security primitives. And the 2008 prices are reusable for any bridge income strategies play that touches fraud, KYC, or cold-email reputation.
